- first_column_row_labels_preview: different sizes (A) and surface-modified PLGA NPs (B). Figures in the bottom are the results of nanoparticles' uptake mechanism in the presence, of single inhibitor for different sizes of bare PLGA NPs (C) and NPs with different surface properties (D)., that PEG-PLGA-150 NPs, with 170 nm size and nearly 0 mV, zeta potential, showed nearly the same uptake level as 100 nm, bare PLGA NPs and twice the uptake level of 200 nm bare, PLGA NPs. This suggests that surface charge is also an, important determining factor, in addition to size, for the, cellular uptake of nanoparticles. It should also be noted that, among all groups of nanoparticles, PEGylated nanoparticles, https://doi.org/10.1021/acsbiomaterials.3c01144 438, ACS Biomater. Sci. Eng. 2024, 10, 429−441

- first_column_row_labels_preview: bromide (MTT) assay indicated that incubating cells with nanoparticles for 24 h, at concentrations up to 100 μg/mL caused only mild toxicity (70−100% cell, viability). Cellular uptake studies were conducted using an in vitro model, developed with a monolayer of HCECs integrated with simulated mucosal, solution. Evaluation of nanoparticle uptake revealed that energy-dependent, endocytosis is the primary uptake mechanism. Among the different nanoparticles studied, 100 nm PLGA NPs and PEG-PLGA-150, NPs showed the highest levels of uptake by HCECs. Additionally, uptake studies in the presence of various inhibitors suggested that, macropinocytosis and caveolae-mediated endocytosis are the dominant pathways. While clathrin-mediated endocytosis was found to, also be partially responsible for nanoparticle uptake, phagocytosis did not play a role within the studied ranges of size and surface, chemistries. These important findings could lead to improved nanoparticle-based formulations, ocular diseases.
